module_mood_contract: Satisfied compounding — 'it runs while I sleep.' Persistence + automation turn a one-shot into a system. Ownership is the payoff. Any edit that makes M2 feel like a set-up-the-tools module steals the compounding feeling.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Mood contract named and honoured.
Epistemic mood ≠ punitive mood.
Lead with the discipline, not the failure mode.
Strategy-fidelity check — no front-running of downstream teaching moments.
rules_evaluated.evidence: building-agent-systems.md line 76: Homework section reads 'Read selected sections from What is an Agent on tools, memory, and context.' The compounding lecture (the M2 closer) already resolves 'why plain text' and introduces the three-layer model (sources → memory → rules file). The homework assigns supplementary/what-is-an-agent.md on 'tools, memory, and context' — the same vocabulary — BEFORE M3, which owns the multi-agent architecture teaching moment. Cross-module risk: if what-is-an-agent.md explains tool orchestration or multi-agent patterns in depth, it front-runs M3's felt learning. Requires reading what-is-an-agent.md to confirm scope; flagged as strategy-fidelity risk pending that read.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Confirm what-is-an-agent.md's homework section assignment is scoped to M2-appropriate vocabulary only (persistence, single-agent, memory) and not tool orchestration or multi-agent patterns. If the assigned sections include M3-owned concepts, narrow the homework pointer.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Strategic themes recur in Key Concepts.
rules_evaluated.evidence: building-agent-systems.md lines 41-44 (Key Concepts): Three bullets — three layers, plain text beats databases, persistence + automation = system. These are tactical. None carry a theme breadcrumb (rule 6 companion). The Bootstrap recurring themes include 'compounding' and 'context shapes output.' The 'context shapes output' throughline (established in M1) is not echoed in Key Concepts. The Compounding lecture names it explicitly ('This is the same mechanism from Module 1: context shapes output, run at system scale') but it doesn't land in the module-level Key Concepts spine.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Add one Key Concepts bullet that surfaces the 'context shapes output — now at system scale' throughline from M1, with a breadcrumb like 'Remember: the loop is the product.' Keeps the tactical bullets but adds the strategic echo.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Key Concepts shape — theme breadcrumbs, not a standalone section.
rules_evaluated.evidence: building-agent-systems.md lines 41-44: All three Key Concepts bullets are plain tactical statements with no bolded breadcrumb. 'Persistence + automation = system. Neither alone is enough.' is the closest to a theme phrasing but is not formatted as a breadcrumb. Per rule 6, 1-3 bullets should carry a short bolded hook.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Add a bolded breadcrumb to the 'Persistence + automation = system' bullet — e.g., '**Remember: either alone is a toy.**'

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Meta-frame lectures are closers, not openers.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Compounding lecture is the closer — placed after build-your-challenge-memory, before Key Concepts. Correct. Demo: 1st scheduled agent is a ceiling-show opener (shows what's possible before exercises). Correct placement.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Minimal lecturing before contrast-mood exercises.
rules_evaluated.evidence: The pre-exercise material in 'Start here' is three rhetorical questions plus a connector demo (5 min). No named practitioner terms, no framework lecture. The opener arms without teaching. Minimal dosage maintained.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Walk-away test (M2+): student leaves the chair while agent works.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Phase 1 Beat 3 (memory build) is an explicit walk-away: plan mode approved, Claude writes 5-8 files autonomously. Phase 4 maintenance pass is another. Two walk-away moments in the anchor exercise. Trace phases 3 and 4 confirm agent writes files autonomously.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Forcing functions live in prompts, not body prose.
rules_evaluated.evidence: build-your-challenge-memory.md Phase 3 compound section: The body prose says 'Drop the link, path, or attachment after the colon at the end of the prompt.' The compound prompt's open-hook colon ('New source:') is correctly inside the fence, but the INSTRUCTION to fill it in is outside the fence in body prose. A speed-runner who reads only prompt-to-prompt will paste the prompt with an empty 'New source:' line — Claude will ask for the source, which recovers, but the designed one-paste flow breaks. Per rule 16, the forcing function (fill in the source) must be visible to prompt-skimmers. The open-hook colon pattern (check_prompts rule 1) handles this IF the action lead-in names the fill-in: the current lead-in does not explicitly say 'paste your source URL after the colon before sending.' building-agent-systems.md Debrief section lines 64-65: body prose describes what the CLAUDE.md should cover ('at minimum: the memory … the sources … the agents') — this is a forcing function for the Debrief content, but it's in the body paragraph around the prompt, not inside the fenced block. A speed-runner copies the Debrief prompt, sends it, and skips the minimum-coverage list.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: For the compound prompt: move the 'drop your source after the colon' instruction into the action lead-in sentence immediately above the fenced block, not into body prose. For the Debrief prompt: move the 'Cover at minimum: memory, sources, agents' list into the fenced block itself.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Classroom is the default; design for follow-along + trainer-cued conversation pauses.
rules_evaluated.evidence: build-your-challenge-memory.md is a 45-minute anchor exercise with four phases. The module body (building-agent-systems.md) has no authored trainer-cue beat between exercises — the transition from name-your-challenge to build-your-challenge-memory is 'Keep the same session running for the next exercise.' There is no authored pause beat where the trainer can check room status, let stragglers catch up, or call a brief discussion. Phase 1 of the anchor exercise runs 20+ minutes with multiple beats (curation conversation, ingest wait, plan-mode approve, audit prompt) without a designed pause. Per curriculum/CLAUDE.md §Classroom delivery: 'A 25-minute exercise without a single designed conversation pause is a draft, not a finished exercise.'
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Add one trainer-cue beat in the module body between the exercises (e.g., 'Trainer asks: who's got a challenge pinned? Show of hands.' after name-your-challenge closes). Add at least one designed pause inside the anchor exercise's Phase 1 — the natural point is after Beat 2 (ingest complete) before Beat 3 (plan mode) where the agent wait is the natural pause anchor.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Between-module reading appears in two places.
rules_evaluated.evidence: building-agent-systems.md line 76 (Homework after Module 2): links agent-trigger-list.md, what-is-an-agent.md, personal-agent-homework.md, and module-3-prework.md. Module 3 file would need to be checked to confirm these readings appear in M3's Prework section as well. This is a cross-file verification; flagged as unverified rather than confirmed PASS. Also: the M2 Prework section (line 8) lists module-2-prework.md items but does not list any items from 'Homework after Module 1' (if M1 has between-module readings that should appear here). Symmetry not confirmed.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Verify that all readings listed in 'Homework after Module 2' appear identically in multi-agent-systems.md Prework section. Verify that M2 Prework mirrors any 'Homework after M1' readings from getting-going.md.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Offered structures need a forced-engagement mechanic.
rules_evaluated.evidence: build-your-challenge-memory.md Phase 1 Beat 3: After plan mode runs and Claude writes 5-8 memory files, the student is told 'Claude returns a plan. Read it. Does the topic split match how you actually think about the challenge? If two topics should be one, say so.' This is invitation prose (body), not a forced engagement mechanic. ~90% of students will approve the plan as-is because a 6-item structured plan with good headers reads as competent. No prompt or forced step asks the student to engage critically before approving. The approval gate exists (plan mode) but the prior engagement step ('read it and push back') is only in body prose and will be skipped by speed-runners. Per rule 32: bare approval gates do not produce real reading.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Before the plan-mode approval gate, add a short prompt or inline instruction that forces one engagement move: e.g., add to the Beat 3 prompt body 'Before you approve: suggest one topic to merge or one missing topic. Even if the plan looks right, name one thing.' This is a low-cost forcing function that survives the approval-inflation pattern.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: When task selection drives the learning outcome, name floor AND ceiling explicitly.
rules_evaluated.evidence: name-your-challenge.md explicitly names bad examples (done re-org, self-improvement, learning goal) and good examples (pricing redesign, EU market entry, AI strategy). Both floor and ceiling are named. The anti-pattern callout is present and specific.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: No placeholders inside fenced blocks; open-hook only when student input genuinely required.
rules_evaluated.evidence: build-your-challenge-memory.md Phase 3 compound prompt ends with 'New source:' (open-hook colon). This is the correct pattern per rule 1d. However: the action lead-in above the fence reads 'Pick a source that fills a gap: a practitioner article you've been meaning to read, a doc you skipped in Beat 2, an email thread, a local file you can share with Claude. Drop the link, path, or attachment after the colon at the end of the prompt.' This lead-in is technically correct but the instruction 'Drop the link… after the colon' is separated from the colon by the intervening fence — it reads as prose before the fence, not immediately at the cursor position. The student reads the lead-in, then the fenced prompt, and the structural relationship between the lead-in instruction and the colon at the bottom of the fence is easy to miss on first read in a classroom setting where they're also watching the trainer.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Tighten the action lead-in to a single sentence that names the fill-in clearly: 'Pick one source that fills a gap and paste the URL or path after the colon.' Keep current prose about what counts as a good source but place it before the action lead-in, not between it and the fence.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Every prompt block gets a one-sentence action lead-in.
rules_evaluated.evidence: build-your-challenge-memory.md Beat 2 (ingest): The prompt block is preceded by 'Now Claude pulls the content into sources/. Agent does the heavy lifting; you don't copy-paste.' This is 2 sentences, not one, and neither is a command-verb lead-in summarising what the prompt does. The lead-in should be: 'Ask Claude to pull every source from the curation plan into sources/ using the best method per source type.' Also: the close prompt ('Using my memory and the rules in the agent file, answer this question, citing which memory file each claim came from:') — its lead-in is 'Run it through the agent you built in Phase 2. That's the one that reads the memory and cites sources.' Two sentences, no command verb, and contains an aside. Not a clean action lead-in. Name-your-challenge.md source-preview prompt lead-in: 'Scan the list. Some suggestions will be obvious; some will surprise you.' — this is a post-prompt instruction used as a lead-in, which inverts the canonical shape.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Audit every prompt block's lead-in for the canonical shape: one sentence, command verb, under 20 words. Three specific fixes: (1) Beat 2 ingest, (2) close prompt, (3) name-your-challenge source-preview.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Canonical presentation shape — lead-in, label with destination, blank line, fenced block.
rules_evaluated.evidence: All prompt blocks in the module and anchor exercise use '**Prompt** *(Claude Code)*' format with no '*(end of prompt)*' closer. Runtime fork spans use the correct rt-code/rt-cowork pattern. No blockquotes or inline backtick wrapping.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Markdown shout inside fenced prompt blocks is dead weight.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Scanning the fenced prompt bodies in build-your-challenge-memory.md and name-your-challenge.md: no **bold** or *italic* inside fences. Bullets and numbers used for structure. Path-shaped strings use backticks appropriately.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Runtime-fork prompt blocks: paired divs, shared chip.
rules_evaluated.evidence: build-your-challenge-memory.md uses rt-code/rt-cowork spans and divs correctly. Beat 3 plan mode instruction is wrapped in rt-code/rt-cowork divs. The main prompt blocks share a single **Prompt** *(Claude Code)* chip. No per-fork chips or forks with different pedagogical content.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Artifact destinations are explicit subpaths, never naked filenames (Bootstrap).
rules_evaluated.evidence: build-your-challenge-memory.md: multiple prompts write to sources/<slug>.md, memory/<topic>.md, memory/index.md, memory/soft-pages.md, agents/<filename> — all paths are explicit subpaths. PASS for these. However: the name-your-challenge.md prompt writes to './challenge.md' with the explicit './' prefix — PASS (deliberate root save with visible intent per rule 23). The Debrief prompt in building-agent-systems.md says to write 'CLAUDE.md at the training-directory root' — the prompt body does not specify './CLAUDE.md' inside the fence; it says 'write the first version of CLAUDE.md at the training-directory root.' Without the './' prefix, this is technically a naked filename in a prose description (not inside a fence, but the fence's instruction is 'write the first version of CLAUDE.md'). Compare with the canonical './CLAUDE.md' pattern from rule 23. Minor: the fenced Debrief prompt says 'write the first version of CLAUDE.md at the training-directory root' — not './CLAUDE.md'. Ambiguous whether Claude would write it at root or CWD.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Inside the Debrief fenced prompt block, change 'write the first version of CLAUDE.md at the training-directory root' to 'write the first version of ./CLAUDE.md'. The './' makes the intent explicit and harness-testable.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Chain prompts by back-reference and deterministic path.
rules_evaluated.evidence: build-your-challenge-memory.md Phase 2 use-it prompt: 'Read the agent file you just created, apply its role and rules, and use my memory. Ask me for the specific task, then do it.' This relies on Claude inferring which file in agents/ is 'the one you just created.' No filename or path given. If Phase 2 creation produced agents/board-memo-agent.md and the student picks a short name, this works via scrollback. But if another agent exists in agents/ from a prior session, or if the session was cleared, Claude may pick the wrong file or create a new one. No deterministic path anchor. Close section also lacks a path for the agent file (see earlier flag).
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Add a back-reference anchor: 'Read the agent file you just created in agents/ and apply its role' OR instruct the student in the action lead-in to note the filename Claude chose, so the next prompt can reference it by name.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Question-dump default — 'in turn' is too soft for one-at-a-time rhythm.
rules_evaluated.evidence: build-your-challenge-memory.md Phase 2 agent-creation prompt: 'Ask me these one at a time: 1. [job], 2. [rules]...' Uses 'one at a time' phrasing. name-your-challenge.md: 'Ask me these in turn: 1. In one sentence... 2. What have you already tried... 3. Where are you currently stuck...' Uses 'in turn' — flagged by rule 15 as too soft. However, the name-your-challenge prompt has only 3 questions and the trace shows Claude asked them sequentially in practice. The risk is lower with 3 questions than with longer lists. Borderline: 'in turn' should be 'one at a time, wait for my answer before asking the next' per the rule.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: In name-your-challenge.md, change 'Ask me these in turn' to 'Ask me these one at a time — wait for my answer before asking the next.'

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Minimal hand-off + Claude self-augments for send-off prompts.
rules_evaluated.evidence: The Beat 3 memory-build prompt uses plan mode as the control point, not an exhaustive prompt. The prompt gives task + paths + non-negotiable rules (3 rules) — this is close to the minimal hand-off shape. Not a walk-away send-off; Claude asks for approval before acting, which is the right control for a first-time memory build. N/A for strict send-off judgment; design is appropriate for M2's trust-distance level.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Mood lands: 8+/10 at every beat.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Phase scores: [8, 8, 8, 9, 8]. Close: 8. No beat below 8.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Mood doesn't resolve early — M3 unease must not be pre-empted.
rules_evaluated.evidence: M2 closes on 'satisfied compounding.' The Debrief/Compounding lecture does not introduce quality concerns, verification concerns, or multi-agent coordination concerns. The unease that seeds M3 (synthesized answer that works well enough to be useful and not quite well enough to trust) is not touched. The 'I wonder if this is right?' question belongs to M3; M2's close stays in 'it runs while I sleep.' PASS with one note: the Compounding lecture's 'The point is simple' paragraph ('Every module after this leans on the memory you just built. Multi-agent systems reads from it. Security reviews it.') previews M3-M4-M5 in one sentence. This is a bridge hint, not a resolution — it doesn't install the M3 unease, but it names that modules exist beyond M2. Low risk; PASS.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Teaching moment lands: the compounding moment (Phase 3 — pages sharpen, claim dropped) reliably triggers across persona skill variation.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Phase 3 is structurally forced: the compound prompt requires Claude to report 'three pages that got sharper (not longer) and one claim that got dropped or replaced.' The reporting mechanic means the teaching moment is observable regardless of how the memory actually changed. The persona who rubber-stamps Phase 3 without reading still has the evidence surface produced by Claude. The moment that could be missed: 'Chat literally cannot do this' (body prose, Phase 3 close). This is in body, not in a prompt, so speed-runners may skip it — but the felt evidence (pages sharpened) is already in Claude's report, and the Compounding lecture names it explicitly. The teaching moment is resilient.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Big Idea fidelity: 'A system remembers, grows, and compounds. Chat doesn't. Your next big challenge is the foundation.'
rules_evaluated.evidence: The Big Idea is honoured. The module's arc — pin the challenge, build the memory, see it compound, write the rules file — is a direct enactment of 'remembers, grows, compounds.' The Compounding lecture's 'Two words, held together' section names persistence + automation as the mechanism. Key Concepts line 3 ('Persistence + automation = system') echoes the Big Idea directly. The 'your next big challenge is the foundation' claim is enacted via the name-your-challenge opener making the challenge selection the first act of the module.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Module-to-module arc: M2 close hands off to M3 open.
rules_evaluated.evidence: building-agent-systems.md 'Next' section (line 70): 'You have an agent doing real work on your challenge. But the job is getting too big for one agent. What do you split?' This directly seeds M3's Multi-Agent Systems frame. The strategy doc confirms M3's open hook is the question of splitting work across multiple agents. The handoff is explicit and uses M3's vocabulary ('split'). PASS.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Phase boundaries land at conversation-pause beats (classroom default).
rules_evaluated.evidence: Same evidence as check_pedagogy.md rule 27: the 45-minute anchor exercise (build-your-challenge-memory.md) has four phases with no authored pause beats in the surrounding module prose. Phase 1 alone spans Beat 1 (curation conversation, ~8 min), Beat 2 (ingest wait, ~5 min), Beat 3 (plan mode approval + file writes, ~7 min) plus a self-audit prompt — 20+ minutes without a designed room-cohesion moment. The transition from name-your-challenge to build-your-challenge-memory is 'Keep the same session running' with no pause. Building-agent-systems.md does not author pause beats between exercises or between phases; it sequences exercise links with inline prose only.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Add trainer-cue beats at natural pauses: (1) after name-your-challenge before launching build-your-challenge-memory — room comparison of challenges; (2) inside Phase 1 after Beat 2 ingest completes — 'while plan mode runs, trainer asks: what surprised you in the source-preview list?'; (3) after Phase 2 agent-use prompt when the memo lands — 'compare your agent's first output with the person next to you.'

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Forcing function in prompts, not body prose (story-class version of check_pedagogy rule 16).
rules_evaluated.evidence: See check_pedagogy.md rule 16 evidence. Additional instance specific to storytelling: build-your-challenge-memory.md Phase 4 maintenance section, body prose after the prompt reads 'Go through Claude's six proposals. Some will be right. Some will miss. Reject those. The ones you approve, Claude applies.' The critical forcing function — 'approve or reject each one' — is partially encoded in the prompt ('ask me to approve or reject each one') but the student framing ('Some will be right. Some will miss.') is entirely in body prose that speed-runners skip. A student who pastes the prompt and rubber-stamps all six misses the pedagogical move (selective trust). The prompt says 'ask me to approve or reject each one' which is correct — but there is no forcing function for the student to think critically before responding, only body prose telling them to do so.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Add a second prompt or inline forcing-function at Phase 4: ask Claude 'which of these six proposals do you have lowest confidence in?' before the student approves/rejects. Forces engagement without being paternalistic.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: M2 density check — strategy doc open TODO: 'Module 2 one-big-idea density.'
rules_evaluated.evidence: The content-strategy.md open TODO reads: 'Module 2 one-big-idea density. Stated big idea: a system remembers, grows, and compounds. Module 2 actually teaches persistence, custom-agent-as-markdown, plan mode, three-layer architecture, self-maintenance, crux naming. Six moves. Some hang on the big idea; some are orthogonal.' The module file confirms this: LOs list Build (memory), Construct (agent), Use (plan mode), Analyze (three layers), Evaluate (compounding vs. growing). Five distinct LOs, not all tightly under one Big Idea. Plan mode is the clearest orthogonal: it's a platform feature, not a compounding concept. The Big Idea doesn't cover 'Use plan mode.' The Debrief crux-naming is also not covered by the Big Idea. The module is front-loaded with capabilities that dilute the compounding throughline.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: This is the strategy doc's own identified risk. From the story judge's lens: plan mode teaches a control mechanism (student agency over multi-file writes), not compounding itself. If plan mode stays in M2, it needs a tighter Big Idea or a reframing that folds it into the compounding concept. Alternatively: relocate plan mode to M3 or prework as a mechanics primer, keeping M2 purely on persistence + automation + compounding.
